one_line_takeaway: A multi-agent LLM framework with a scenario-decomposed 'Targeted Analysis' mode markedly outperforms both zero-shot GPT and ten traditional static/symbolic/fuzzing tools on smart contract vulnerability detection, especially on real-world complex-logic bugs where classic tools score zero.
key_finding: The two prompting/agent strategies substantially lift LLM detection over zero-shot, with TA mode strongest on hard cases. On the labeled dataset BA (GPT-3.5) reaches 74% overall recall, beating the best traditional tool (Mythril, 54%). TA converts near-total failures into near-perfect scores (e.g., GPT-3.5 TOD F1 rises from ~0 zero-shot to 94.7%; GPT-4o TOD from 46.2% to 100%). On real-world Code4rena data all three traditional tools (Slither, Mythril, Conkas) detect 0 of the reported bugs, whereas TA mode detects 147/304 specific (48.36% recall) and 525/1,104 complex-logic (47.55%) vulnerabilities, for 672/1,408 overall (47.73%). The system also flagged 11 previously unreported vulnerabilities across 4 types, at a claimed max cost of ~1 USD per contract.
core_contribution: LLM-SmartAudit, a multi-agent conversational framework that automates smart contract security auditing by orchestrating four specialized LLM-powered agents (Project Manager, Smart Contract Counselor, Auditor, Solidity Programming Expert) through a three-phase task queue: Contract Analysis, Vulnerability Identification, and Comprehensive Report. The framework's own novelty lies in two operational strategies: Broad Analysis (BA) mode, built on a ReAct-style Thought-Reasoning prompt for wide-spectrum scanning, and Targeted Analysis (TA) mode, built on a Buffer-of-Thoughts-style Buffer-Reasoning prompt that decomposes vulnerability identification into 40 scenario-specific detectors. It also introduces a role-swapping/consensus mechanism (capped at n=3 rounds) to reduce single-agent false positives. Note: David et al., GPTScan, LLM4Fuzz, and Chen et al. are cited prior GPT-based works, NOT this paper's contribution; the authors position their framework as broader in scope and multi-agent where those are single-query or single-agent.
threat_model: Defensive tooling for the smart contract setting. The security concern is exploitable vulnerabilities in Ethereum/Solidity smart contracts (e.g., reentrancy, integer overflow/underflow, transaction-order dependence, unsafe delegatecall, tx.origin authorization, unsafe suicide, gas limitation) that, because deployed contracts are immutable, cannot be patched post-deployment and can be exploited by malicious actors to drain funds (paper cites >3.24B USD in losses Apr 2018-Apr 2022 and the DAO attack). LLM-SmartAudit is the defender's detector, aiming to surface these bugs pre-exploitation more cheaply than human audit firms (which start at ~500 USD and reach 5,000-15,000 USD). The paper does not study adversarial manipulation of the LLM auditor itself (e.g., prompt injection in contract code); its adversary is the on-chain exploiter, not an attacker against the tool.
limitations: BA mode remains weak on context-heavy vulnerability types such as TOD, unsafe suicide, and gas limitation, attributed to the base model's limits. Effectiveness is tightly coupled to the underlying LLM (model dependence), and the TA mode's 40 scenarios are not exhaustive, leaving emerging/novel bug classes hard to catch. The approach relies on static analysis and cannot capture dynamic vulnerabilities. Evaluation datasets are modest (110 labeled cases) and the labeled benchmark is binary/annotated by the authors. Comparisons to prior GPT tools (David et al., GPTScan) use those works' published statistics rather than re-runs due to unavailability. The 11 newly found bugs were submitted to Code4rena for verification but not confirmed at publication. Real-world evaluation restricted to GPT-4 due to token limits, so BA/TA GPT-3.5 real-world numbers are absent. The paper's front matter contains boilerplate template artifacts (mismatched CCS concepts/keywords, placeholder DOI).

datasets_used: Labeled dataset: 110 annotated contract cases in 11 sub-datasets covering 10 specific vulnerability types (RE, IO, USE, UD, TOD, TM, RP, TX, USU, GL) plus one secure-contract sub-dataset

Real-world dataset from Code4rena contests: 102 projects, 6,454 contracts (499 high-risk, 909 medium-risk, 1,420 low-risk, 2,417 ground-level vulnerabilities); study focuses on 1,408 high+medium bugs = 304 specific + 1,104 complex-logic
quantitative_results: Labeled set overall recall: BA/GPT-3.5 74%, Mythril-0.24.7 54%, Slither-0.10.0 46%, Conkas 44%, ConFuzzius 42%, sFuzz 33%, Oyente 23%, Securify 18%, GNNSCVD 15%, Eth2Vec 13%, VeriSmart 9%
GPT-3.5 TA-mode F1 vs zero-shot: TOD 94.7% (from ~0), GL 94.7% (from 50%), USE 95.2% (from 70.6%), RP 100% (from 82.4%)
GPT-4o TA-mode F1: TOD 100% (zero-shot 46.2%), IO/UD/TM/RP/TX/USU 100%, GL 94.7% (zero-shot 62.5%)
Real-world recall: TA mode specific 147 TP/48.36%, complex-logic 525 TP/47.55%; BA mode specific 92 TP/30.26%, complex-logic 110 TP/9.96%; Slither/Mythril/Conkas 0 TP/0.00% on both
Real-world overall: 672 of 1,408 vulnerabilities detected (47.73% recall)
RQ4: 11 newly discovered vulnerabilities across 4 types (e.g., Unlimited Token Approval in SushiYieldSource.sol) not in prior audit reports
Cost: max ~1 USD per contract vs human audit firms starting ~500 USD (Trail of Bits 5,000-15,000 USD)
baselines_compared: Zero-shot GPT prompt baseline (GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4o)
Prior GPT-based works via published stats: David et al. (recall 39.73%, 58/146; ~2,000 USD cost) and GPTScan (40 TP of 232 across 72 projects)
